text:
Jamaica’s Shelly-Ann Fraser-Pryce pulled out of theOlympics100m semi final on Saturday due to injury after a late rule change disrupted her preparations.

The 37-year-old, who has won three Olympic gold medals and is the most decorated 100m sprinter of all time, is making her last-ever appearance at the Games in Paris.

Fraser-Pryce came through her 100m heat in second place with a time of 10.92 seconds on Friday, but ahead of her semi final on Saturday night, which was scheduled to start at 18.59pm, the acronym ‘DNS’ – which stands for ‘did not start’ – was next to the Jamaican sprinter’s name.

Before the race, Fraser-Pryce was denied access to the Stade de France at the perimeter security gate due to a last-minute rule change which was applied to all athletes attempting to gain entry to the stadium on Saturday.

Athletes who do not stay at the Olympic Village and arrive on the team bus have been ordered to take a different route. Fraser-Pryce was among the athletes who walked from the entrance gate to the stadium.

In a video which circulated on social media, Fraser-Pryce said: ‘She [a security member] said they changed the rule yesterday.

TF change rules!???pic.twitter.com/DcMrhOYvoq

‘How can you change the rule and then not say? So you’re asking all athletes who, for whatever reason don’t stay at the village, they can’t come through the gate.

‘We came through this gate yesterday and went through security and it was okay. They want us to go all the way up to where everybody is exiting… that’s crazy!’

Fraser-Pryce has also been suffering from a knee injury before the start of the track events in Paris and was seen training with strapping to her right knee during a session last week.

Jamaica’s team manager, Ludlow Watts, later revealed that Fraser-Pryce had withdrawn from her semi final due to injury.

‘When we got the news she was warming up and so it came as a surprise to me,’ said Watts.

‘We only got the information that she is injured, but doctor Warren Blake is addressing the matter and we will speak further on the matter.

‘This was a surprise and disappointment because the whole world would love to see Shelly in the Olympics. Apart from being a Jamaican, I am sure that people who love track and field would be very disappointed globally.’

With one lane left clear due to Fraser-Pryce’s absence, Saint Lucia’s Julien Alfred ended winning the semi final with a time of 10.84 seconds. The United States’ Sha’Carri Richardson, who is the world champion, finished second in 10.89 seconds.

Alfred went on to win the 100m final later on Saturday evening with a time of 10.72 seconds. Richardson took silver in 10.87, while her fellow United States sprinter Melissa Jefferson got the bronze medal as she finished in 10.92.

Alfred’s win is Saint Lucia’s first-ever medal in the history of the Olympics.

‘I’m thinking of God, my dad, who didn’t get to see me,’ Alfred said after her historic win.

‘He passed away in 2013. Dad, this is for you. I miss you. I did it for him, I did it for my coach and God.’

Alfred also revealed she drew inspiratioin from Usain Bolt ahead of her 100m victory on Saturday.

‘Usain Bolt won so many medals, I went back this morning and watched his races,’ she said.

‘I’m not going to lie, it was all Usain Bolt’s races this morning.’

After the news of Fraser-Pryce’s withdrawal was revealed, Dame Jessica Ennis-Hill said during theBBC’s coverage: ‘That’s really sad.

‘For Shelly-Ann Fraser Pryce not to be able to complete the series in the way she wanted to – she will be devastated.’

Fraser-Pryce first appeared at the 2008 Olympics in Beijing, winning the 100 meters in 10.78 seconds. She then successfully defended her Olympic crown at London 2012 as she won gold once again with a time of 10.75 seconds.

At the Olympics in Tokyo three years ago, Fraser-Pryce was part of the Jamaica team that won gold in the women’s 4x100m relay.

Fraser-Pryce has also won five Olympic silver medals as well as a bronze. At the World Athletics Championships, she has won 16 medals in total, including 10 golds.

Elsewhere, Team GB’s Dina Asher-Smith failed to qualify for the final after finishingfifth in her semi final.

For the second successive Olympics, Asher-Smith, who is the British record holder, has failed to progress from the semi-final stage.

‘I’m just disappointed because I’m in great shape and have been in great shape all season, I fully expected to make that final, the race wasn’t even fast,’ Asher-Smith said after the race.

‘I know I’m in a lot better shape than that.

‘I just go on to the 200m, but again I’m fully disappointed. I should have made that.’

title: Shelly-Ann Fraser-Pryce out of Olympics 100m final after late rule change | Metro News
description: Jamaica's Shelly-Ann Fraser-Pryce has pulled out of the Olympics 100m semi final on Saturday.
